title: Iran's Internal Politics and the Future of the IRGC
content:
For hardline factions within the Iranian regime, maintaining power on their own terms is absolutely paramount. These conservative elements are deeply energized by their profound anger at Israel and the United States, an animosity that has grown exponentially during recent conflicts. However, not everyone within the government wants Iran’s future to simply mirror its tumultuous and isolated past. Many reform-minded officials recognize that aggressive policies have led to disaster, and they are increasingly willing to push for a different trajectory. In the midst of the ongoing war, the Iranian president even urged the powerful Revolutionary Guards to preemptively address the country's dire economic situation.

Prying control away from the Revolutionary Guards will undoubtedly be difficult given their immense coercive capacity and deeply entrenched influence. Yet, while their relative power has increased during the recent military conflicts, their absolute authority has actually been severely diminished. It was the Guards' aggressive strategy that pushed Iran to the brink of defeat, bankrupted the economy, and alienated vast swaths of the population. This disastrous mismanagement has cost the military corps significant internal political capital and eroded its base of public support. Furthermore, the war has ravaged its ranks, eliminating many of its most capable and experienced security personnel.

Meanwhile, the country's most competent reform-minded leaders have mostly been spared from the recent violence. This surviving group includes several former presidents and pragmatic parliamentary managers who possess enough institutional clout to enact meaningful change. These officials are highly canny political operators who could effectively exploit the newfound looseness in Iran’s political regime. By working behind the scenes to unify the state, they hope to galvanize public support for a more moderate path forward. If these leaders can present a clear plan to stabilize the economy and ease social pressures, the powerful military might struggle to suppress them.
